did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep neural networks, specifically InceptionV3 as a backbone with Support Vector Classifiers (SVCs) and softmax, for multiclass skin disease classification on dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018 dataset. It also employs image preprocessing techniques like segmentation, dilation, and cropping to enhance classification performance.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, specifically evaluating the effects of different preprocessing methods and classifiers (InceptionV3 with SVCs and softmax) on the ISIC 2018 dataset. It reports new accuracy metrics (76.32%) and compares training strategies (scratch vs. transfer learning), which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Multiclass Skin Disease Classification within Dermoscopic Images Using Deep Neural Networks
journal: 12th International Symposium on Information and Communication Technology (SOICT 2023)
brief_summary: This study addresses the task of multiclass skin disease classification using dermoscopic images to assist in early skin cancer diagnosis. The authors employed InceptionV3 as a deep neural network backbone, combined with Support Vector Classifiers (SVCs) and softmax, on the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. The methodology involved evaluating various preprocessing techniques, including hair removal, image augmentation, and a specific segmentation strategy involving dilation and cropping. The main evaluation approach focused on comparing the impact of these preprocessing methods and classifier types on classification accuracy. The study achieved a maximum accuracy of 76.32% using the segmentation with dilating and cropping strategy. The results conclude that proper image preprocessing significantly enhances model performance, demonstrating the potential of deep learning to support dermatologists in repetitive diagnostic tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 7 classes in the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset used for multiclass classification. Training set count: 261 images (3.26%).

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Benign keratosis (seborrheic keratosis and/or lichen-like keratosis)

One of the 7 classes in the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset used for multiclass classification. Training set count: 92 images (1.15%).

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin disease classes (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin diseases using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories from the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset. The study evaluates classifiers (Softmax, Linear SVC, RBF SVC) combined with an InceptionV3 backbone to assign images to one of seven specific lesion classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC 2018 Challenge focusing on computer-aided diagnosis and melanoma detection using dermoscopic images. It contains high-resolution images of various skin lesions acquired from diverse clinical settings and imaging devices.

use.training: Used for training the InceptionV3 model with preprocessing (hair removal, augmentation, segmentation with dilation and cropping). The original training set was split 80-20 to create the training and validation sets.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (2005 images derived from the 80-20 split of the original training set).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (1512 images).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance by weighting the loss function inversely proportional to class frequencies, ensuring rare classes have equal impact on the result.
To visualize and evaluate the number of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives for each class.
To measure the proportion of correctly classified samples from the total number of samples.
To calculate the harmonic mean of precision and recall for each class individually and then take the average, evaluating the model's ability to classify all classes regardless of size.
To calculate the mean of recall scores for each class separately, ensuring the model performs well for each class without bias from class distribution.
To calculate the F1 score for each class and then take a weighted average based on class distribution, addressing dataset imbalance.
To halt the training process when the validation loss ceases to improve, preventing overfitting.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using accuracy, F1 scores, and Balanced Multiclass Accuracy metrics without statistical significance testing.

experiment_1_comparison_training_strateg.description: Comparison of training from scratch vs. transfer learning using 3 classes (AKIEC, BCC, BKL) for initial evaluation.
experiment_1_comparison_training_strateg.notes: Training from scratch gave significantly higher results than transfer learning.

experiment_2_preprocessing_and_classifie.description: Evaluation of different preprocessing techniques combined with Softmax, Linear SVC, and RBF SVC classifiers. All models trained from scratch.
experiment_2_preprocessing_and_classifie.notes: The model trained with linear SVC on the segmented dataset exhibited the best performance. Augmentation method attained the highest weighted F1-score of 77% (with Softmax or RBF SVC). Linear SVC consistently achieved the highest results in most cases compared to other classifiers.

competition_comparison.description: Comparison of the proposed method's BMA score against top models from the ISIC 2018 Competition.
competition_comparison.notes: The proposed method did not surpass the latest updated methods from the competition but demonstrated potential. The best competition method achieved a BMA of 0.885.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights initialized from the ImageNet dataset

architecture_details.feature_extractor: Used as an automatic feature extractor; transforms image inputs into feature vectors

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named (standard Keras default assumed)
training_details.learning_rate: Initial 3e-4, reduced to 1e-4 due to loss fluctuations
training_details.epochs: At least 40 epochs, with early stopping applied after the 40th epoch when validation loss ceased to improve
training_details.loss_function: Weighted Focal Loss (WFL) to address class imbalance; formula: WFL(pt) = -αt(1-pt)^γ log(pt)
training_details.hardware: Kaggle Kernels with GPU P100 accelerator (15.9GB GPU memory, 13GB RAM)

classifiers_evaluated: Radial Basis Function Support Vector Classifier (RBF SVC)
classification_or_output_head.softmax_head_details: Added three fully connected layers, including a 3-class softmax layer at the end (note: text mentions 3-class for initial evaluation, but task is multiclass); feature vectors linearly combined to produce outputs
classification_or_output_head.svc_head_details: C-Support Vector classifiers provided by scikit-learn; uses hyperplanes to separate data points

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Radial Basis Function Support Vector Classifier (RBF SVC)
preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using Dull Razor software (adaptive median filtering to smooth and replace hair pixels)
Image augmentation (brightness adjustment, rotation, flipping, shearing, and zooming with randomness)
Segmentation using polar transformation (center-point predictor heatmap and polar network for binary mask)
Cropping based on dilated segmentation mask to retain lesion and background information

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training dataset to improve data diversity. Techniques included brightness adjustment, rotation, flipping, shearing, and zooming, all applied with a level of randomness.

conclusions: Proper image preprocessing, specifically segmentation with dilation and cropping, significantly enhances the performance of deep learning models in multiclass skin disease classification, achieving a maximum accuracy of 76.32%.
Training the InceptionV3 model from scratch yields significantly higher classification results compared to using transfer learning with frozen feature extractors.

Linear Support Vector Classifiers (Linear SVC) consistently outperform Softmax and Radial Basis Function (RBF) SVC classifiers when paired with the InceptionV3 backbone across various preprocessing strategies.

The proposed method achieved a Balanced Multiclass Accuracy (BMA) of 0.61, which is lower than the top-performing models from the ISIC 2018 Competition (highest BMA 0.885), indicating that while effective, the method did not surpass state-of-the-art ensemble approaches.

Deep learning techniques have significant potential to assist dermatologists in repetitive diagnostic tasks, although manual inspection remains prone to errors due to the similarity between lesions and healthy tissue.
Addressing class imbalance through weighted focal loss is necessary to ensure that rare classes have an equal impact on the model's training and to improve the reliability of findings.
important_remarks: The proposed method achieved a Balanced Multiclass Accuracy (BMA) of 0.61, which is significantly lower than the top-performing models from the ISIC 2018 Competition (highest BMA 0.885), indicating it did not surpass state-of-the-art ensemble approaches.

Training the InceptionV3 model from scratch yielded significantly higher classification results (e.g., 76% accuracy) compared to using transfer learning with frozen feature extractors (e.g., 62% accuracy) in initial evaluations.

The study addresses severe class imbalance in the ISIC 2018 dataset (e.g., NV class at 66.97% vs. DF at 1.15%) by employing Weighted Focal Loss, but does not report performance metrics specifically stratified by class to demonstrate effectiveness on rare classes.

The model was evaluated exclusively on the ISIC 2018 Task 3 dataset; there is no external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of generalizability to diverse populations or skin types.
The study is limited to seven specific skin disease classes; it does not address other common dermatological conditions or the full spectrum of skin cancer subtypes.

The preprocessing pipeline relies on specific segmentation masks (polar transformation) and hair removal (Dull Razor), which may introduce bias or failure modes if the segmentation algorithm performs poorly on challenging images.

The hardware setup (Kaggle Kernels with GPU P100) and software libraries (TensorFlow, scikit-learn, Keras) are specified, but hyperparameter tuning details for the SVC classifiers (e.g., C parameter, gamma) are not explicitly reported in the summary.

The paper acknowledges that manual inspection by dermatologists is prone to errors due to the similarity between lesions and healthy tissue, but does not provide a direct clinical comparison or inter-observer agreement study to validate the CAD system's utility against human experts.
